content.text: I now have comprehensive, authoritative information from the official Claude Code documentation and well-sourced practitioner guides. Let me write the full tutorial.Creating custom subagents is one of the most powerful ways to extend Claude Code: rather than asking Claude to do everything in a single conversation that grows increasingly bloated, a subagent delegates a bounded task to a separate Claude instance with its own context window, its own tool permissions, and its own system prompt—returning only the summary that the main session actually needs.

Subagents are specialized AI assistants that handle specific types of tasks. Use one when a side task would flood your main conversation with search results, logs, or file contents you won't reference again: the subagent does that work in its own context and returns only the summary. Define a custom subagent when you keep spawning the same kind of worker with the same instructions.

Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions. When Claude encounters a task that matches a subagent's description, it delegates to that subagent, which works independently and returns results.
The practical value is in that separation. Instead of reading 30 files into the main thread, the main session spawns a subagent ("find every place that calls
and summarize the call sites"), and the subagent does the heavy reading in its own context. The main conversation receives only the distilled result, not the volume that produced it.
Claude Code includes built-in subagents that Claude automatically uses when appropriate. Each inherits the parent conversation's permissions with additional tool restrictions.
The three primary built-ins cover the most common delegation needs:

subagent is a fast, read-only agent optimized for searching and analyzing codebases, running on Haiku for low latency. Claude delegates to Explore when it needs to search or understand a codebase without making changes. This keeps exploration results out of your main conversation context. When invoking Explore, Claude specifies a thoroughness level: quick for targeted lookups, medium for balanced exploration, or very thorough for comprehensive analysis.

subagent is used during plan mode. When you're in plan mode and Claude needs to understand your codebase, it delegates research to the Plan subagent so that exploration output stays in a separate context window while the main conversation remains read-only.

subagent handles work that requires both reading and writing. Claude delegates to general-purpose when the task requires both exploration and modification, complex reasoning to interpret results, or multiple dependent steps.
Explore and Plan skip your CLAUDE.md files and the parent session's git status to keep research fast and inexpensive. Every other built-in and custom subagent loads both.
Beyond these built-ins, the full architecture of custom subagents is what this article covers.
Creating a Subagent: File Format and Location
Subagents are defined in Markdown files with YAML frontmatter. You can create them manually or use the

---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code for quality and best practices
tools: Read, Glob, Grep
model: sonnet
---

You are a code reviewer. When invoked, analyze the code and provide
specific, actionable feedback on quality, security, and best practices.

The frontmatter defines the subagent's metadata and configuration. The body becomes the system prompt that guides the subagent's behavior. Subagents receive only this system prompt (plus basic environment details like working directory), not the full Claude Code system prompt.
The following diagram shows how scope, location, and priority relate:
When multiple subagents share the same name, the higher-priority location wins. The precedence order from highest to lowest is: managed settings (organization-wide),
) are ideal for subagents specific to a codebase. Check them into version control so your team can use and improve them collaboratively.
recursively, so you can organize definitions into subfolders such as
. The subdirectory path does not affect how a subagent is identified or invoked, because identity comes only from the
values unique across the whole tree: if two files within one scope declare the same name, Claude Code keeps one and discards the other without warning.
One important operational note: subagents are loaded at session start. Edit a file on disk and you have to restart the session to pick up the change. Agents created or edited through
take effect right away. That asymmetry catches everyone once.
The interactive path to creating a subagent is the

command, which opens a tabbed interface. The Running tab lists live and recently finished subagents and lets you open or stop them. The Library tab lets you view all available subagents (built-in, user, project, and plugin), create new subagents with guided setup or Claude generation, edit existing subagent configuration and tool access, delete custom subagents, and see which subagents are active when duplicates exist.

The guided creation flow walks through name, description, tool selection, model selection, display color, and optional persistent memory configuration, then writes the resulting file to disk and makes it available immediately.
are required. All other fields are optional:

---
name: security-reviewer
description: Scans code changes for security vulnerabilities. Use when reviewing
  PRs, after changes to auth or payment code, or when the user asks for a
  security audit.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
disallowedTools: Write, Edit, Bash
model: claude-opus-4-8
permissionMode: plan
maxTurns: 20
skills:
  - owasp-checklist
memory: project
background: false
effort: high
isolation: worktree
color: red
---

You are a senior security engineer conducting a focused security review.
Analyze the provided code or diff for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities,
secrets in code, injection risks, and authentication issues.
Output: severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low), location, and remediation step.
Never modify files. Return only the security analysis report.
The key fields and their effects:
must use lowercase letters and hyphens. Hooks receive this value as
. The filename does not have to match.

is the routing mechanism. Claude matches the user's request against subagent descriptions to decide whether to delegate automatically. Write it like a routing rule: describe the exact phrases and situations that should invoke this agent, including "use proactively" if you want Claude to reach for it without being asked.
restricts the tool allowlist. The subagent inherits all tools if this field is omitted. To preload Skills into context, use the
removes specific tools from whatever is inherited or specified. This is useful for ensuring a "reviewer" agent cannot inadvertently write files even if the main session permits it.
. When Claude invokes a subagent, it can also pass a
parameter for that specific invocation. Claude Code resolves the subagent's model in this order: the
environment variable if set, the per-invocation
frontmatter, and finally the main conversation's model.
sets the subagent's permission stance. Options are
. This field is ignored for plugin subagents.
caps the number of agentic turns before the subagent stops, which prevents runaway loops on complex tasks.

preloads specific skills into the subagent's context at startup. The full skill content is injected, not just the description. Subagents can still invoke unlisted project, user, and plugin skills through the Skill tool.
grants access to specific MCP servers. Each entry is either a server name referencing an already-configured server (e.g.,
) or an inline definition with the server name as key and a full MCP server config as value. This field is ignored for plugin subagents.
defines lifecycle hooks scoped to this subagent only, covered in more detail below.
enables persistent memory across sessions, with scope
always runs this subagent as a background task. The default is
overrides the session effort level for this subagent. Options are

gives the subagent its own temporary git worktree. This provides an isolated copy of the repository branched by default from your default branch rather than the parent session's HEAD. The worktree is automatically cleaned up if the subagent makes no changes.
sets the display color in the task list and transcript. Accepts
is auto-submitted as the first user turn when the agent runs as the main session agent via
Defining Subagents via the CLI Flag
For quick testing or automation scripts, subagents can be defined inline at launch time without creating files:
CLI-defined subagents exist only for that session and aren't saved to disk, making them useful for quick testing or automation scripts. Use
for the system prompt, equivalent to the markdown body in file-based subagents.
Programmatically defined agents take precedence over filesystem-based agents with the same name.
Claude decides whether to run subagents in the foreground or background based on the task. You can also press Ctrl+B to background a running task.

The distinction matters for how permissions are handled. If a background subagent needs to ask clarifying questions, that tool call fails but the subagent continues. If a background subagent fails due to missing permissions, you can start a new foreground subagent with the same task to retry with interactive prompts.

in a subagent's frontmatter ensures it always runs in the background. This is appropriate for research tasks and analysis work that should not block the main session. Use foreground mode (the default) for operations that may need interactive permission confirmation mid-run.
To disable all background task functionality, set the
Subagents can accumulate knowledge across sessions using the

---
name: codebase-architect
description: Deep codebase analysis agent with accumulated knowledge. Use when
  architecture questions arise or when exploring unfamiliar areas of the codebase.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
model: opus
memory: project
---

You are a codebase architect. Over time, accumulate knowledge about this
project's architecture patterns, key abstractions, and recurring conventions.
On each invocation, load your memory notes, perform the requested analysis,
and update your notes with new insights.

Memory location: .claude/agent-memory/codebase-architect/
field enables persistent memory with scope
For subagents that need to make experimental file changes without touching the main working copy,
runs the subagent in a separate git worktree:

---
name: experimental-refactor
description: Runs refactoring experiments in an isolated worktree. Use when
  trying a risky or uncertain refactoring approach.
tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash
model: sonnet
isolation: worktree
background: true
---

You are a refactoring specialist working in an isolated environment.
Make the requested changes, run the tests, and report the results.
Your changes are in a separate worktree and won't affect the main codebase
until the results are reviewed and merged.
enables fork mode, where every subagent spawn runs in the background regardless of the
field. Forks still surface permission prompts in your terminal as they occur; named subagents auto-deny anything that would prompt.
Hooks defined in a subagent's frontmatter are scoped to that subagent's lifecycle, providing deterministic enforcement of constraints without affecting the main session:

---
name: db-reader
description: Execute read-only database queries. Use when production data
  needs to be inspected for debugging or analysis.
tools: Bash
hooks:
  PreToolUse:
    - matcher: "Bash"
      hooks:
        - type: command
          command: "./scripts/validate-readonly-query.sh"
---

You are a database read specialist. Execute only SELECT queries.
Never run INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP, or DDL statements.
Report all findings in a structured format with row counts and sample data.

field defines lifecycle hooks scoped to this subagent. This field is ignored for plugin subagents. This makes it possible to enforce constraints at the system level—not relying on the model's compliance with instructions—for operations where safety is critical.

Claude invokes subagents automatically when a user's request matches a subagent's description. This is the primary invocation mechanism for well-described agents. The description is the routing rule, so it should contain the phrases users actually type.
For explicit invocation, subagents can be named directly in conversation:
Subagents provided by an enabled plugin appear in the typeahead under their scoped name, such as
. Named background subagents currently running in the session also appear in the typeahead, showing their status next to the name. You can also type the mention manually without using the picker:
followed by the scoped name for plugin subagents.
To run an entire session as a specific subagent, the
flag replaces the default Claude Code system prompt with the subagent's:
The subagent's system prompt replaces the default Claude Code system prompt entirely, the same way
files and project memory still load through the normal message flow.

Each subagent invocation creates a new instance with fresh context. To continue an existing subagent's work instead of starting over, ask Claude to resume it. Resumed subagents retain their full conversation history, including all previous tool calls, results, and reasoning. The subagent picks up exactly where it stopped rather than starting fresh. When a subagent completes, Claude receives its agent ID. The built-in Explore and Plan agents are one-shot and return no agent ID, so they can't be resumed; use general-purpose or a custom subagent when you need to continue the work.

As of Claude Code v2.1.172, subagents can spawn their own subagents. A subagent five levels below the main agent cannot spawn further subagents, regardless of whether it runs in the foreground or background. To prevent a subagent from spawning others, omit
from its tools array or add it to

This enables orchestration patterns where a high-level planning subagent spawns multiple specialist subagents in parallel, each working on a component of a larger task and returning independent summaries.
Forks are a distinct mechanism from named subagents. The differences are significant:
Fresh context with only the passed prompt

Because a fork's system prompt and tool definitions are identical to the parent, its first request reuses the parent's prompt cache. This makes forking cheaper than spawning a fresh subagent for tasks that need the same context. Named subagents start fresh and are better suited for well-defined, bounded tasks.
What a Subagent Receives at Startup

A custom subagent does not receive the full Claude Code system prompt. It receives its own system prompt from the markdown body, plus basic environment information (working directory, available tools). It also loads
files and project memory unless those are explicitly skipped. Skills listed in the
frontmatter field are fully preloaded at startup rather than loaded on-demand.

The only channel from parent to subagent is the Agent tool's prompt string, so include any file paths, error messages, or decisions the subagent needs directly in that prompt. The parent receives the subagent's final message verbatim as the Agent tool result, but may summarize it in its own response.

---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code changes for bugs, security issues, and style violations.
  Use proactively after implementation changes, before committing, or when the
  user asks for a review.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
disallowedTools: Write, Edit, Bash
model: sonnet
color: blue
---

You are a senior code reviewer. Analyze the code or diff provided and give
specific, actionable feedback organized by severity.

Output format:
## Critical (blocks merge)
## Warning (should fix)
## Suggestion (nice to have)

For each item: cite file, line, problem, and recommended fix.
Keep total feedback under 500 words. Do not modify files.

---
name: test-runner
description: Runs the project test suite and returns a structured summary of
  failures. Use when tests need to be checked after code changes.
tools: Bash, Read
model: haiku
maxTurns: 10
background: true
---

You are a test execution specialist. Your job is to run the test suite
and return a structured report of any failures.

Steps:
1. Detect the test framework from package.json, pytest.ini, or similar
2. Run the tests: npm test / pytest / go test / etc.
3. Collect all failures
4. Return ONLY: total tests, passed, failed, and a list of failed test
   names with their error messages

Do not attempt to fix failures. Do not return full test output.
Return the structured summary only.

---
name: security-audit
description: Comprehensive security audit of code changes. Use before merging
  PRs touching authentication, payment, or data access code. Use when the user
  requests a security review or mentions OWASP or CVEs.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash(git *)
disallowedTools: Write, Edit
model: claude-opus-4-8
effort: high
isolation: worktree
color: red
---

You are a senior security engineer conducting a focused audit.

Analyze for:
- OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
- SQL/NoSQL/command injection
- Authentication and authorization flaws
- Secrets or credentials in code
- Insecure configurations
- Dependency vulnerabilities (check package manifests)

For each finding: severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low), file and line,
description, and remediation step.

End with a risk summary: overall rating (Clean/Low/Medium/High/Critical)
and the three most important findings to fix first.

---
name: commit-message
description: Generates a conventional commit message from staged changes.
  Use when ready to commit, when the user asks for a commit message, or
  when they say "commit this" or "what should I commit?"
tools: Bash(git *)
model: haiku
maxTurns: 3
---

You are a git commit message specialist. Generate a conventional commit
message for the staged changes.

Steps:
1. Run `git diff --cached --stat` to see what's staged
2. Run `git diff --cached` to see the actual changes
3. Determine type: feat/fix/chore/refactor/docs/test/perf/ci
4. Determine scope from file paths
5. Write a subject line: max 72 chars, lowercase, no period at end

Return ONLY the commit command to run, in this format:
git commit -m "<type>(<scope>): <subject>"
Without it, the subagent inherits all tools from the parent session. A "reviewer" that can also write files is not a reviewer—it's a full agent. Always specify
explicitly for subagents with constrained roles.

The description field is the difference between a subagent that gets used and one that sits idle. Claude picks which subagent to invoke automatically by matching the user's intent against the description field of every available agent. The algorithm is not magic—it's a model judging description-versus-intent fit. Write descriptions that contain the exact phrases users are likely to type.
Too much in the system prompt.

A tight system prompt for a code-reviewer is typically 30 to 60 lines of markdown. Longer than that and you're probably duplicating what should be in CLAUDE.md (project-level conventions belong there, not in every agent). Shorter than that and the agent either over-interprets the prompt or asks the main session for clarification—which defeats the point of delegation.
Not restarting the session after editing a file.
files on disk are only picked up at session start. The
interface bypasses this. If a subagent isn't reflecting recent edits, restart the session.
Inheriting broad permissions for sensitive operations.
Letting a subagent inherit the main session's full toolset, including Bash without restrictions, can lead to unintended consequences. Ship every agent with an explicit
For security reasons, plugin subagents do not support the
frontmatter fields. These fields are ignored when loading agents from a plugin. If you need them, copy the agent file into
If two files within the same scope (e.g., two files in
in frontmatter, Claude Code keeps one and discards the other silently. Filenames don't matter—only the
Write descriptions as routing rules, not labels.

Include the phrases users will actually type and the conditions that should trigger the agent. "Expert code reviewer" is a label. "Reviews code for quality and best practices. Use proactively after code changes, before committing, or when the user says 'review this' or 'check my changes'" is a routing rule.
Commit project subagents to version control.

Project-level agents should define the team's shared specialists—the reviewer, the test runner, the documentation writer. These get committed to the repo so every engineer works with the same agent definitions.
Use model routing for cost control.

Route fast, bounded tasks to Haiku (test running, summarization, classification). Use Sonnet for code review and analysis. Reserve Opus for deep reasoning, complex architecture work, and security audits where thoroughness matters more than speed.
Background agents that encounter an unexpected situation will keep trying rather than surfacing permission prompts. A
cap prevents runaway token consumption when something goes wrong mid-task.

Any agent that modifies files during an experimental or risky task should work in a worktree. The worktree is automatically cleaned up if the subagent makes no changes. This makes the main working copy a safe baseline that isn't affected by subagent experiments.

After defining a subagent, phrase several natural requests that should and should not trigger it. If the agent isn't being picked up automatically, the description needs more specific trigger phrases or the phrasing isn't matching what users actually say.
A custom subagent is a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter stored in
are required; all other fields are optional.
The markdown body becomes the subagent's system prompt. Subagents receive this prompt plus basic environment info, not the full Claude Code system prompt.

field is the auto-invocation routing mechanism. It must contain the specific phrases and conditions that should trigger the agent—vague descriptions produce agents that are never automatically invoked.
control what a subagent can and cannot do. Always specify these explicitly for agents with constrained roles; omitting
gives a subagent its own temporary git worktree, protecting the main working copy from experimental changes.
enables cross-session learning, allowing a subagent to accumulate knowledge about the codebase over time.
Subagent files edited on disk only take effect after a session restart; the
—copy the definition into a local scope if those fields are needed.
As of v2.1.172, subagents can spawn their own subagents up to five levels deep, enabling hierarchical orchestration of complex tasks.
